title: Now, even Russia's most elite hackers are using Clickfix to infect devices
gist: Ukraine's CERT-UA reports that Sandworm, the GRU's elite military-intelligence hacking unit, has adopted the Clickfix fake-CAPTCHA social-engineering technique — previously mostly used by financially motivated criminals — to compromise Ukrainian organizations, using a novel Ethereum-smart-contract-based method to dynamically serve malicious payloads and evade takedown.

themes.theme: Nation-state adoption of criminal attack techniques

insights.claim: CERT-UA reported that Sandworm, an advanced hacking unit inside Russia's GRU military intelligence, has adopted the Clickfix technique — tricking victims into copying and running a malicious script via a fake CAPTCHA — a method that emerged roughly a year ago and had previously been used primarily by financially motivated criminal hackers.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Demonstrates technique diffusion from cybercrime to top-tier nation-state actors, a pattern that typically precedes broader adoption of Clickfix-style attacks against Western government and critical-infrastructure targets beyond Ukraine.

insights.claim: The Sandworm Clickfix campaign, active since spring 2026, compromised at least one organization's network via the FreakyPoll Python backdoor and involved at least 10 identified malicious websites serving fake PowerShell-based CAPTCHA prompts.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete scale marker (10+ compromised sites, confirmed network breach) for the current campaign that defenders and threat-intel platforms should track and cross-reference against their own indicators of compromise.

insights.claim: The campaign uses a custom program called SmartAxe that dynamically obtains the domain name of the attacker's remote payload server by making an Ethereum smart-contract call (eth_call), allowing the malicious CAPTCHA content to be served from constantly-changing domains that are harder to blocklist.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: A novel blockchain-based command-and-control resilience technique that raises the bar for domain-blocklist-based defenses; security vendors should prioritize behavioral/script-execution detection over static domain reputation for this threat class.

insights.claim: Sandworm's malware toolkit in this campaign includes GhettoVibe (a VBS startup-directory loader), ScoutCurl (a PowerShell reconnaissance script), FreakyPoll (a Python backdoor), FluidLeech (malware disguised as antivirus software), LoadLoop, and an Android backdoor called CowardDuck delivered via fake app-install lures.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Provides specific malware-family names and functions that security teams and threat-intel subscribers should ingest into detection signatures given confirmed nation-state use.

insights.claim: Sandworm previously relied primarily on booby-trapped pirated software distributed via torrent trackers and extended Signal conversations to lure targets into installing malware disguised as security software, showing it is now diversifying its initial-access techniques.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Confirms an active shift in a top-tier state actor's tradecraft, useful context for security vendors updating detection coverage beyond legacy Sandworm TTPs.
insights.locator: penultimate paragraph on prior Sandworm tactics
